sentences.text: I just looked up the date and I see I made, I want to make a correction.
The German attack on Poland I stated was 1940 September the 1st.
This is the wrong date.
The right date was September the 1st, 1939, not 1940.
Yes, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in-- in all this turmoil, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- I-- I learned, I saw at that time that adversity affected people entirely different ways.
In one way, people that were not very religious before all of sudden, the adversity brought out of the-- brought out-- they became all of sudden very religious.
And those that were religious before, lived a religious life, it had the opposite effect on them.
And I'll tell you why.
This is what reminded me.
So people started to stop and to-- to pray on the field but by the field, the highway was.
We weren't too far from the highway.
All of a sudden, we noticed German military, in military trucks, were going somewhere.
They were going up.
And then, all of a sudden, one guy that was a very religious guy, a [HEBREW], from a small community.
I knew the community but I didn't know the man.
And he started yelling and screaming, he says, do you-- do you want to-- to draw attention to yourself?
He had the tallit on, you know.
There are Germans.

We kind of, even though the Hungarians did all that but we kind of were scared more of the Germans than we were scared of the-- and at that time, I remember that this man said, he said, I laid [HEBREW].
You know, very religious Jews would lay two different kinds of [HEBREW], not one.
You know what [HEBREW] is.
They would lay one kind of [HEBREW] and [INAUDIBLE] until after the [HEBREW] and then they would take these [HEBREW] off and put on another set of [HEBREW].
One is the set that's [HEBREW], and these are called [HEBREW].
That's not important right now.
But very, very pious and religious Jews would do that.
Most of them would not do it.
I mean, I used to see that, for instance, my father wouldn't do that.
But I used to see we had our rabbi, the [HEBREW], you know, those that were making a living in the congregation as religious leaders, they would observe these things.
And he started screaming, he said, I'm sorry, I'm sorry all my life that I laid two pairs of [HEBREW].
Throw the [HEBREW] away.
Throw the tallit away.
And he was starting like-- like blasphemy.
And I looked at that and this is-- this is the type of Jew that would say something.
It turned out, he found out that he somehow lost his daughter and a child that was-- that was-- he escaped and he lost in a-- in a few days.
How he got to our group, I don't know.
Maybe he came a day later.
Or I don't know exactly.
Anyway, that's why-- why I-- I said that it had-- now, these-- this woman and the two daughters and the son that were-- were living in Budapest, they were completely assimilated.
And all of a sudden, they didn't know anything about Judaism.
I remember, they started-- started asking us.
It had an opposite effect on them.
So anyway, the, the-- the conflict was resolved that most of the people stopped praying and we started-- we started going-- going in the direction back to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH].
There were small children and-- and after a few hours walking on the highway in single lanes, they got tired.
The good fortune was that it was summer and there was no rain and it was warm outside in August.
So we decided we'll take a rest.
To take a rest, we laid-- laid again off from the highway into a field.
Everybody had his little [? package ?,] his whatever he had with him.
And we made ourselves comfortable, so to speak.
And then it got already dark.
We decided we'll continue to-- to-- our walk next-- next-- next day.
We stayed on that field.
It was-- it was already 8, 9, 10 o'clock at night.
We could hear moans and groans but we-- we-- we were-- how many we were in there, I think, roughly a couple hundred people were in there.
But the local Ukrainian guys, they saw-- watched our movement.
They saw which way we are going.
And we, of course, we didn't know that.
So they, they-- they devised a-- a plot how to get-- we all had packages, how to get all this.
When we were-- we were making nighttime in that group on the field, they surrounded us, our group, and they started a barrage of stones.
Throwing at us at the night in that group.
They figured we were-- everybody will run away.
Anyway, all of sudden, the night-- the quietness broke open.
Some kids and some people got busted their heads with stones and tremendous screams broke out and pandemonium broke out.
And we didn't know where it's coming, and it's a hail of stones.
I, myself, got one in the back, too.
We jumped up and we started to-- to run, to run to the highway.
But it was a very dark, pitch dark night.
We run to the highway.
In meantime-- in the meantime, we realize, we-- three of us, Moshe and the children, are not here.
So we started running back.
And while we were running back, we met up, we met up with them.
And then we realized that we left everything-- we left everything there, whatever we had, in those-- so we-- we told Moshe and Monte, well, the-- the screams.
Where is [NON-ENGLISH.
[NON-ENGLISH] was missing until they found him.
And we told them to stay here.
We are going back, [NON-ENGLISH] and I will go back.
We run back and fortunately we found our packages, we grabbed them, and we started running back to the highway.
And while we were already the six of us together, we took-- and-- and-- and and-- and-- and fortunately the kids were not hit and they were so quiet.
They were so quiet.
Up to this day, I can't understand it, how these children, they knew something is wrong in all this.
And we started to run back of the same highway from where we came.
How-- we ran for a few minutes until we had to stop and to catch our breath until we noticed-- from far away we noticed, that there is a barn on the field.
So we decided we are going.
And here, we are only the six of us.
The three brothers, my sister-in-law and the two children.
We are going and tried to go into this-- to this barn.
We saw a house there, a barn.
So we came in there.
The barn was open.
I mean, it was-- it wasn't locked.
We were able to open it up and we came in.
There was straw in-- in the place.
We fell down with our-- with our poor [NON-ENGLISH] that we had with us and from-- from great exhaustion and-- and-- and I remember, I fell-- we fell asleep.
What happened to the rest of the people, we never saw them again.
We never know what happened to them.
It-- it must have been midday, next day.
It must have been midday.
And we heard a-- a-- a lot of clattering, awesome wagon clattering coming closer, closer.
Coming and stopped at that barn.
He opened the door.
And a guy comes in and he saw us there and he says [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH].
What are you doing here?
We started to-- to plead with him and explain to him that we were attacked here and-- and we had nowhere else to go and-- and-- and so that we came in here.
And we said we would-- we would pay him if he would take us to the next little place that is called [?
Istichka. ?] Because we found that out from the previous place, from the mayor's sons that there is a small village called [?
Istichka. ?] There are Jews in there.
So we figured we wanted to get to, more or less, to a Jewish community.
And so he saw-- he saw that we had-- we had a woolen blanket rolled up that we had-- we had with us.
And he said he would take us there if we give him that blanket and a pair of under-- underpants.
So we agreed to that.
We'll give him the blanket.
We'll give him the underpants.
He said, it's only about five, six kilometers from here, this [?
Istichka. ?] But he said he wouldn't do that now.
He will wait until it gets darker in the evening hours, 7, 8 o'clock, you know, summertime.
And he let-- let us stay there, and he-- and he left.
Of course, we were kind of leery.
We didn't know if-- if he is going to keep his-- his promise but we had no choice.
We had no other choice but to wait.
And we stayed there and by 7 o'clock or so he did come.
He did come.
We gave him the agreed price, the blanket and-- and a pair of underpants.
And he put-- he put Moshe, my sister-in-law, and the two children on the wagon and we walked alongside.
And he brought us to a place called [?
Istichka. ?] At the outskirt of the place there was a Jewish widow that lived there and evidently he knew her and that was the closest for him and he dumped us off over there.
That widow lived with a daughter and son-in-law in that house.
And they were very gracious.
They took us in.
They had a room and they took us into their-- to their house.
And we found out that in this place, [?
Istichka. ?] There is-- there is Hungarian troops come and go there and they stay some of them there.
That for Hungarian money, the soldiers, you can buy from them, they would sell cigarettes.
They would sell conserves that they would have.
And cigarettes, that was the currency doing the wartime.
For some reason, for cigarettes you could buy anything.
So anyway, we stayed in this place in [?
Istichka ?] somewhere-- Somewhere between 10 and 12 days.
The-- the two women-- the two women were, they had to go out.
The son-in-law, his name must have been Leif or Levi.
They used to call him, you know, a nickname, [?
Yovichka ?].
I don't know, a funny thing, he must have had some whisky somewhere because it was [LAUGHS] emanating from him all the time.
I don't know in the house.
And he was a terribly nervous person.
His wife would go out and she would buy, already that time you could buy carrots, you could buy tomatoes, you could buy corn on the cob, you know, and they would bring.
So we went out also to buy.
If you could find Hungarian soldiers, we could buy cigarettes and for cigarettes you could-- anyway, a funny thing happened.
This guy was always hungry.
And he used to walk around and we had to have a laugh in all this [INAUDIBLE] and he spoke to him in Yiddish and he was talking like he talks to his stomach.
He would say, [YIDDISH], which translates in English, he says, stomach, stomach, don't bother me.
Do I have it and I don't give it to you?
[LAUGHS] And that made us really laugh but he was-- he was drunk.
He was drinking.
And another thing which was, I have no-- I don't know how and why, a Ukrainian priest would come in every day.
Even he had an eye on that widow.
And he would visit her but he would come every day into the house.
And we were there for a few days, like I said, 10, 12 days.
And my sister-in-law, she would talk to him.
Of course, we spoke Ukrainian, all of us.
And she would talk to him, and she asked him to-- to write us a letter, to give us a letter to whom it may concern, that to please, we are Hungarian from the Carpathian refugees.
And we are trying to go back to our birthplace.
That the local people shouldn't hinder us, that they should help us.
She was pleading with him, with this priest, to give us a letter, that kind of a letter.
And he agreed.
He said he would do it but he noticed that she was wearing a diamond ring.
And he, for that-- for that thing, he wanted that she should give him that diamond ring.
And we talked it over among ourselves and we decided this isn't-- this isn't worthwhile to invest.
I don't know, this was her-- her wedding-- wedding, the ring that she got, diamond.
That she was wearing it on her hand, that was stupid.
That's besides the point.
But that's what it was, she was still wearing it.
[SIGHS]
So what happened was that we decided that this is too high a price to pay.
That if we would give him 100 Hungarian pengos-- this was Hungarian money-- if he would write us such a recommendation letter to-- to-- to [INAUDIBLE].
And he finally-- he finally decided to do that and he gave us a letter.
And, of course, we told him that the [NON-ENGLISH], this was the Ukrainian national movement.
That we know it and the Carpathian-- it wasn't all-- all-- all true that we are so friendly with him.
We were plenty afraid of him but we had no other way of-- of obtaining this letter from him.
We finally obtained that letter from him.
One day, and here I come to the-- one day, the three brothers, three and I and Moshe, decided to go out to the farmers and try to buy some food.
Maybe we can buy either flour or-- or bread or-- or cheese, cottage cheese, milk, something.
And we went out and after awhile we got caught up, stopped by two local militia.
They were called [NON-ENGLISH], local.
They had uniforms.
This was the Ukrainian uniforms.
And, of course, they were with weapons.
And they stopped us and they said, where are you going?
So we said, we are going.
We are here.
They dropped us here from Hungary and we are going.
We are trying to buy some food.
So they ordered us, you go with us.
Do we have a choice not to go?
We went.
And those two militia guys, they brought us into a small village into a Jewish home where there was a man that owned a flour mill.
And this man's name was Isaac [?
Zumer. ?] And that name I will never in my life forget.
He was instrumental in saving our lives, and I'll-- I'll tell you how.
They brought us in to this Isaac [?
Zumer, ?] and they told him, we brought you three Hungarian Jews here.
And they said to him, Isaac, you are responsible for them.
We will come tomorrow.
We will come tomorrow for them, and if they are not here tomorrow, you'll be shorter with a head-- a head shorter, they told him.
And they left.
And they left, this guy, Issac [?
Zumer, ?] he said, you sit down to the table, the three of you.
I'll get you something to eat.
He owned the only lumber mill-- not lumber mill, flour mill-- in the area and he knew all the local people.
He even knew those local militias.
And, of course, he was not allowed to have any more-- the mail officially, but he had a Gentile guy that worked for him, so he was another strongman for him.
But he was not-- he had no shortage of food.

And for the first time in two weeks or three weeks, since we left, I don't remember, for the first time the three of us had a full, satisfying, meat, bread, butter, and-- and-- and-- Are we-- are we close now?
Close, yeah.
Can I go on?
